title: Multi-scale drift detection — reduce false-critical severity from regime changes
hypothesis: The drift detector reports 89.2% critical drift because it uses a single 504/252 day window, which compares data across regime changes. By checking drift at multiple time scales (long/medium/short), we can detect whether the model is tracking recent patterns well even if long-term distributions shifted — reducing false severity and improving signal confidence.
what_i_noticed: 89.2% of features flagged as drifted at critical severity
This single assessment tanks crash model weight to 20% and signal confidence to 17%
The crash model may actually be fine if recent data is stable — a regime change 1-2 years ago doesn't mean the model is broken NOW
The drift detector only checks one time scale (504 ref / 252 inf), which compares data from potentially different market regimes
KS test with synthetic reference reconstruction produces false positives at small sample sizes (< 126 inference samples)
what_i_did: Added from_multi_scale() method to DriftDetector — checks drift at 3 time horizons (long: 504/252, medium: 252/126, short: 126/63)
Implements 'shortest stable scale' logic: if any non-primary scale shows drift ≤ moderate, uses that as effective severity
Added recent_stability classification: stable / degrading / unstable based on best non-primary scale
Added multi_scale_summary narrative for human-readable reports
Optimized: caches full reports to avoid recomputing primary scale twice
Used best non-primary scale (not just shortest) for recent_stability to handle KS false positives at small sample sizes
Wired from_multi_scale into ALL 5 call sites: drift router, crash router (2x), market router, stock router
Added multi_scale_windows config to config.py for tunability
Updated lab data_generator.py to capture multi-scale fields in experiment data
Wrote 20 comprehensive tests covering: structure, severity override, importance weighting, custom scales, edge cases

after.expected_improvement: If medium or short scale shows stable data, effective severity drops from critical → low/moderate, crash weight rises from 0.2 → 0.7-1.0, signal confidence increases proportionally

key_findings: The KS test produces false positives at small sample sizes (63 inference samples) because it reconstructs reference distributions from bin proportions — a lossy approximation that KS then flags as 'different'
The medium scale (252/126) is the most reliable short-term indicator — large enough for accurate KS tests, recent enough to capture current regime
For the synthetic gradual-drift test: long scale shows 100% drift (critical), medium shows 5% (low), confirming multi-scale correctly identifies recent stability
This directly addresses the #1 system issue — 89.2% critical drift tanking all signal confidence across the platform
self_critique: Did not actually run against live market data to verify the improvement on real features — the test uses synthetic data only

The KS test false-positive issue at small windows is a deeper problem that should be fixed (increase bin count, use stored raw reference data, or use a permutation test instead of synthetic reconstruction)
Could have added a frontend component to display multi-scale drift info — stayed backend-only
The from_multi_scale runs from_rolling_window 3 times (once per scale), each of which creates a new DriftDetector — could be optimized to share reference data between overlapping windows
next_steps: Fix KS test synthetic reconstruction to reduce false positives at small windows (store raw reference data or use permutation test)
Add frontend display for multi-scale drift info in the dashboard
Run live validation: call /api/drift/check with real market data and verify effective_severity is more realistic
Consider adaptive PSI thresholds: financial features like momentum/vol have naturally higher PSI than macro indicators
Profile the 3x from_rolling_window calls for performance — may need to optimize for the API hot path
impact: high — directly addresses the #1 platform issue (critical drift → low signal confidence)
